metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `emit_sidecar=False` (and explicitly `sign=False`) to the `review_plan(...)` call in `replay_candidate.py` to prevent experiment runs from writing REVIEW_RESULT sidecar events to the ticket store for historical tickets.
evidence: Plan states: 'Run with REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off and do not sign, so the experiment mutates no shared state.'
review_plan signature: `emit_sidecar: bool = True` (default True)
sidecar.emit() writes REVIEW_RESULT events to .tickets-tracker/<rid>/ for each replayed ticket

Plan's baseline strategy reads via `all_review_results(tid)` (sidecar history) — candidate-run sidecar emissions would append new entries for those tickets, interleaving experiment artifacts with production history
findings.finding: The replay driver is instructed to call `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and 'do not sign', but the plan never instructs passing `emit_sidecar=False`. The `review_plan` signature defaults to `emit_sidecar=True`, so each candidate run will write a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event into the local store for every one of the ~200 historical tickets. This contradicts the plan's own claim that 'the experiment mutates no shared state': it would overwrite the persisted sidecars for replayed tickets, potentially corrupting a subsequent `all_review_results(tid)` call used to re-freeze the baseline or used in production remediation-mode reads for those tickets.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay' paragraph; also review_plan() signature at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:387-399
scenarios: A candidate replay run (without emit_sidecar=False) appends REVIEW_RESULT events for 200 historical tickets; a subsequent 're-freeze' of baseline.jsonl would pick up these experiment-emitted rounds as the 'most-recent plan-v3 round', producing a contaminated baseline.
Production remediation-mode reads for a replayed ticket (via latest_review_result) may see the experiment's sidecar as the newest, affecting the rising-floor novelty-prior set.
findings.suggested_fix: In replay_candidate.py's run subcommand: `review_plan(tid, repo_root=repo_root, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)`. Document this in the Scope section alongside the existing `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` instruction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True` (or equivalent) when calling `review_plan()` in the replay driver so idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh short-circuits are bypassed and a genuine LLM re-run is guaranteed for every sampled ticket.
evidence: review_plan() signature: `sign: bool = True, emit_sidecar: bool = True, force: bool = False` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:395-398)
_run_plan_review: 'if sign and not force: reused = _idempotent_reuse(…); if reused is not None: return reused' (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:508-511)
_idempotent_reuse returns the existing PASS verdict without any LLM call when attestation is still valid (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:438-486)
Cleanly-closed filter guarantees material_fingerprint is unchanged — exactly the condition that triggers idempotent reuse
Plan states 'do not sign' but does not translate this into a `sign=False` parameter in the driver design
findings.finding: The plan calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` as the candidate entrypoint but does not specify `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True`. At default settings (`sign=True, force=False`), `_idempotent_reuse` will short-circuit for every sampled ticket whose existing attestation is still valid — which is the common case for cleanly-closed tickets with unchanged plan material (the exact cohort the plan targets). Those tickets return the baseline verdict verbatim with zero LLM calls, producing a spuriously low flip rate and defeating the candidate replay entirely.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`'
scenarios: On the candidate branch, sampled cleanly-closed tickets still have valid plan-review attestations (material unchanged, code not drifted). review_plan() hits _idempotent_reuse and returns the prior PASS verdict without running any LLM. The transition matrix reads PASS→PASS for all such tickets regardless of R1's prompt change.
Even if some tickets have drifted code, drift_refresh may also short-circuit before running a full review, again suppressing the candidate finder prompt.
findings.suggested_fix: In `replay_candidate.py`, call `review_plan(tid, repo_root=repo_root, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)`. `sign=False` disables both idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh (see `_run_plan_review` guards on `if sign`); `force=True` is an additional defense-in-depth; `emit_sidecar=False` prevents writing candidate findings into the shared sidecar store.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `emit_sidecar=False` to the `review_plan()` call in the replay driver (in addition to `sign=False`) to ensure no REVIEW_RESULT sidecar events are written to the local store during the candidate replay.
evidence: review_plan() default: `emit_sidecar: bool = True` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:396)
_run_plan_review: 'verdict[sidecar_emitted] = sidecar.emit(verdict, …) if emit_sidecar else False' (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:570-572)
sidecar.emit writes a REVIEW_RESULT event file under the ticket dir (replaces latest_review_result for that ticket)
REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off prevents push to remote but does NOT prevent local writes; the sidecar file is still written
Plan's stated goal: 'the experiment mutates no shared state'
findings.finding: The plan states the experiment 'mutates no shared state' via `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and 'do not sign', but does not suppress `emit_sidecar`. With the default `emit_sidecar=True`, every candidate-run review writes a new `REVIEW_RESULT` event file to the executor's local ticket store for each sampled ticket. This overwrites `latest_review_result` and `prior_concerns` for those tickets on that workspace, so any subsequent production reviews of the same tickets (post-experiment) would reason against candidate-branch findings, not the current-build findings.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Run with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and do not sign, so the experiment mutates no shared state.'
scenarios: The executor runs the candidate replay on a workspace that is also the production workspace. After the experiment, a production review of any sampled ticket reads candidate-branch findings via prior_concerns, potentially biasing the next real review.
A crash mid-run leaves a mix of candidate-run sidecars on sampled tickets, corrupting the baseline.jsonl→sidecar correspondence for any future re-run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `review_plan()` call signature in the driver, including `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True` (or equivalent), and explain why `force=True` is needed to bypass `_idempotent_reuse` and `drift_refresh` so the candidate prompt actually executes for every sampled ticket.
evidence: `review_plan()` signature: `sign: bool = True, force: bool = False` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:395,398)

`_run_plan_review`: 'if sign and not force: reused = _idempotent_reuse(…); if reused is not None: return reused' (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:508-511) — returns prior PASS with zero LLM calls when attestation is still valid
The cleanly-closed filter (`post_claim_edit_class=='none'`, `reopen_count==0`, `force_close==false`) guarantees material fingerprint is unchanged — exactly the condition triggering idempotent reuse
The plan notes 'do not sign' in isolation but never translates this into `sign=False` or `force=True` in the driver design; no parameter list is given for the `review_plan` call
findings.finding: The plan calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` as the candidate replay entrypoint but omits the required `sign=False` (or `force=True`) parameter. At default `sign=True, force=False`, `_run_plan_review` invokes `_idempotent_reuse` before running any LLM pass; for cleanly-closed tickets with unchanged plan material (the exact cohort the plan targets), `_idempotent_reuse` returns the prior PASS verdict with zero LLM calls, silently defeating the candidate replay.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`'
scenarios: On the candidate branch, all ~200 sampled cleanly-closed tickets still have valid plan-review attestations (material unchanged). `review_plan()` hits `_idempotent_reuse` and returns the prior PASS for every one. The transition matrix reads PASS→PASS universally regardless of R1's prompt change — the flip rate is 0% trivially, producing a falsely favorable gate result.
Even with `sign=False`, `drift_refresh` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:517-518) may short-circuit before running the candidate finder prompt if the code has not drifted.
findings.suggested_fix: Call `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)` in the driver. `force=True` bypasses both `_idempotent_reuse` (line 508) and `drift_refresh` (line 517), ensuring a full LLM pass runs. `sign=False` and `emit_sidecar=False` prevent the experiment from mutating shared state (which the plan already intends — 'do not sign').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the replay driver calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)` to bypass idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh short-circuits and suppress sidecar mutation; add this parameter set explicitly to the harness spec and the Scope section.
evidence: `review_plan` signature: `sign: bool = True, emit_sidecar: bool = True, force: bool = False` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:395-398)

`_run_plan_review`: 'if sign and not force: reused = _idempotent_reuse(…); if reused is not None: return reused' (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:508-511) — returns existing PASS with zero LLM calls
Cleanly-closed filter (`post_claim_edit_class=='none'`, `reopen_count==0`) ensures material is unchanged — exactly the precondition that triggers idempotent reuse
Plan says 'do not sign' only in the REBAR_SYNC_PUSH context, never as a driver parameter specification
Evidence class: Verified (observed — live code read)
findings.finding: The plan calls `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` for the candidate replay and says 'do not sign,' but never translates this into the explicit parameters `sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True` in the driver design. With the default `sign=True, force=False`, `_run_plan_review` will hit `_idempotent_reuse` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:508-511) for every cleanly-closed ticket whose attestation is still valid — the exact cohort the plan targets — and return the prior PASS verdict with zero LLM calls, bypassing the candidate rubric change entirely. The replay would silently produce baseline-identical verdicts, making the flip-rate and FP-control metrics meaningless.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph: 'Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample via `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`' and 'Run with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and do not sign'
scenarios: Candidate branch replay: sampled cleanly-closed tickets have unchanged plan material and valid existing attestations. review_plan() returns the prior verdict instantly without touching the candidate rubric, producing PASS→PASS for all such tickets regardless of R1's prompt change. The PASS→BLOCK flip rate reads ~0% even if R1 substantially changes findings.
Even for tickets whose code has drifted, `drift_refresh` (also gated on `sign=True`) may short-circuit before the full workflow runs, again suppressing the candidate finder prompt.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'The replay' paragraph, replace the bare `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)` call spec with `review_plan(tid, repo_root=…, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)`. Document the rationale: `force=True` bypasses `_idempotent_reuse` and `drift_refresh`; `sign=False` prevents attestation writes; `emit_sidecar=False` prevents sidecar mutation. These three flags together ensure every sampled ticket runs the full candidate pipeline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the false precedent claim: the `run_review_*.py` harnesses do NOT checkpoint per-ticket with atomic append; cite the actual pattern (e.g., `docs/scale-envelope.md`'s `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` + atomic-write notes, or specify the per-ticket `jsonlines.append` pattern explicitly as new work) so implementers have a grounded reference.
evidence: run_review_children.py: accumulates in `out = {}`, `summary = {}` dicts and writes to a file at the end (no per-ticket atomic append, no resume logic).
run_review_configchildren.py: same pattern — iterates children, accumulates to `out`/`summary`, no checkpointing.

Plan quote: 'iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing' — the atomic-append resume pattern is ABSENT from the cited precedent files.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing' — but reading run_review_children.py and run_review_configchildren.py shows these scripts accumulate results in a dict and write once at the end (or per-subprocess); they implement no per-ticket atomic-append checkpointing and no mid-run resume logic. The cited precedent does not demonstrate the pattern the plan relies on.
findings.location: What section — The replay paragraph: 'The existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing'
scenarios: An implementer reading the cited harnesses for the checkpointing pattern finds none and must design it from scratch, with no in-repo precedent to validate against.
If the author meant a different set of harnesses (e.g., a future E2 harness), the citation is wrong and the design detail is ungrounded.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the existing harnesses/run_review_*.py batch drivers are the precedent: iterate the ticket set, checkpoint per ticket (atomic append)' to 'following the atomic-write pattern (`.tmp` + `os.replace`) used by mine_outcome_corpus.py, checkpoint each ticket result to raw_verdicts.jsonl via atomic append so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing.' This grounds the checkpoint design on an actual in-repo example.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify where the full-sample PASS→BLOCK flip_rate is stored: either add it explicitly as a field of `transition_matrix.json` (and update AC #4's prove command accordingly), or confirm fp_control.json intentionally holds both the full-sample flip_rate and the 30-plan FP-control metrics and document this dual role.
evidence: 'flip_rate = |{PASS→BLOCK}| / |{tickets with baseline_verdict == PASS, included}|' (Primary metric section — denominator is all included PASS-baseline tickets in the ~200 sample)
AC #4: "jq -e 'has(\"flip_rate\") ... fp_control.json'" (30-plan subset file)
AC #5 describes fp_control.json fields as: new_blocking_median, new_advisory_median_delta, null_floor_pass_to_block, replays|length==3, r1_pass — all 30-plan metrics

Scope lists per-candidate files as 'raw_verdicts.jsonl, transition_matrix.json, criterion_deltas.jsonl, criterion_deltas_summary.json, fp_control.json' with no mention of flip_rate as a field of transition_matrix.json
findings.finding: The plan's 'Primary metric' section defines PASS→BLOCK flip_rate over the full ~200-plan cleanly-closed PASS-baseline denominator and states it is 'Recorded per candidate,' but AC #4's proving command verifies `flip_rate` inside `fp_control.json` — the file the Scope and AC #5 describe as holding the 30-plan FP-control subset results. The `transition_matrix.json` (the natural home for the full-sample matrix metric) is not listed as carrying a `flip_rate` field anywhere in the plan. This creates a cross-section contradiction: the metric definition implies full-sample scope; the verification target implies 30-plan scope.
findings.location: What §'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%)'; AC #4 proving jq command; Scope per-candidate file list
scenarios: Implementer reads Primary metric section and writes flip_rate to transition_matrix.json; AC #4's jq check on fp_control.json fails even though the metric is correctly computed.

Implementer writes flip_rate only to fp_control.json (satisfying AC #4) but the 30-plan denominator is used instead of the full ~200-plan denominator, silently reporting an underpowered estimate as the primary metric.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `flip_rate` as a named field in `transition_matrix.json` (alongside the cell counts), update AC #4's jq prove command to point to that file, and reserve `fp_control.json` exclusively for the 30-plan FP-control metrics (AC #5). This aligns the metric's scope (full ~200 sample) with its storage location.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command in AC 2 (or a new AC) that asserts `transition_matrix.json` contains a top-level `flip_rate` field for the full ~200-sample (e.g. `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' …/<label>/transition_matrix.json`), and clarify that `fp_control.json`'s `flip_rate` is the 30-plan subset rate, not the primary full-sample rate.
evidence: 'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%).' — plan body
AC 4 jq: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' …/fp_control.json` — only checks the 30-plan file
AC 2 jq checks matrix arithmetic consistency but not the presence of a `flip_rate` field in `transition_matrix.json`
ABSENCE: no proving command reads `flip_rate` from `transition_matrix.json`
findings.finding: AC 4's proving commands only validate `flip_rate` in `fp_control.json` (the 30-plan FP-control subset), but the plan designates the full ~200-sample PASS→BLOCK flip rate as the PRIMARY metric with an epic bar of ≤2%. Neither AC 2 nor AC 4 includes a proving command that asserts a `flip_rate` (or equivalent) field exists in `transition_matrix.json` for the wider sample, leaving the primary metric's storage location unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4; 'What' section: 'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%)'
scenarios: An executor produces `transition_matrix.json` without a `flip_rate` key (just raw cell counts), making the primary metric unretrievable without a separate ad-hoc computation — AC 2 passes green but the primary gate metric is absent.
A reviewer inspecting `fp_control.json` conflates the 30-plan flip rate with the full-sample ≤2% bar, misreporting the gate result.
findings.suggested_fix: In `e3_metrics.build_matrix` output, add a top-level `flip_rate` key (= pass_to_block / included_pass_baseline) to `transition_matrix.json`. Add to AC 2's proving block: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/transition_matrix.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k fp_control -q` golden test to AC 5 that asserts `fp_control_eval` returns `r1_pass=True` for a fixture with `new_blocking_median=2, new_advisory_median_delta=0` and `r1_pass=False` for `new_blocking_median=4, new_advisory_median_delta=2`, and update AC 5's proving block to include this command.
evidence: AC 5 jq: `has("r1_pass")` — presence-only check, no value assertion
Scope lists `fp_control_eval` in `e3_metrics.py` as a pure function — ideal for a golden unit test
AC 3 pytest: `-k delta`; AC 4 pytest: `-k flip` — no pytest k-expression covers `fp_control_eval`
ABSENCE: no AC asserts `r1_pass == True` when `new_blocking_median <= 3` and `new_advisory_median_delta <= 1`, or `False` otherwise
findings.finding: AC 5's jq proving command only checks that `r1_pass` EXISTS as a field in `fp_control.json` (`has("r1_pass")`), but does not verify that its boolean value is correctly derived from `new_blocking_median` and `new_advisory_median_delta`. No pytest test covers the `fp_control_eval` function (AC 3's `-k delta` covers `criterion_deltas`; AC 4's `-k flip` covers `pass_to_block_flip_rate`; no `-k fp_control` or equivalent covers `fp_control_eval`). The correctness of the R1 gate decision itself — the primary output of E3 — is untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 5; Scope: `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval`; `tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py`
scenarios: A bug in `fp_control_eval` sets `r1_pass = True` unconditionally; the jq existence check passes green; R1 is incorrectly green-lit and promoted to production.
The median computation uses mean instead of median due to a copy-paste error; structural AC passes; gate decision is wrong.
findings.suggested_fix:
In `tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py`, add:
```python
def test_fp_control_eval_pass():
    result = fp_control_eval(replays=[...], null_floor=0)
    assert result['r1_pass'] is True
    assert result['new_blocking_median'] <= 3
    assert result['new_advisory_median_delta'] <= 1

def test_fp_control_eval_fail():
    result = fp_control_eval(replays=[...], null_floor=0)
    assert result['r1_pass'] is False
```
Add `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k fp_control -q` to AC 5's proving block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a golden test case (e.g., `-k fp_control`) in `test_e3_metrics.py` that exercises `fp_control_eval` with a small fixture covering: (a) the happy path where all thresholds pass, (b) a case where `new_blocking_median > 3/30` flips `r1_pass` to False, and (c) a case where `new_advisory_median_delta > 1` flips it to False.
evidence: AC 4 names `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k flip -q` and proves `pass_to_block_flip_rate` only
AC 5 uses `jq -e 'has("new_blocking_median") and has("new_advisory_median_delta") …'` — a schema check, not a behavioral assertion
The plan lists `fp_control_eval` as a named pure function in `e3_metrics.py` alongside `build_matrix`, `criterion_deltas`, `pass_to_block_flip_rate` — all pure and testable offline
ABSENCE: no `-k fp_control` or equivalent golden test is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: `fp_control_eval` — the function that computes the R1 gate decision (`new_blocking_median`, `new_advisory_median_delta`, `r1_pass`) — is not covered by any named test in `test_e3_metrics.py`. The AC golden tests name `-k delta` (criterion_deltas join) and `-k flip` (pass_to_block_flip_rate arithmetic) but omit a `-k fp_control` case. The jq AC for `fp_control.json` is a schema presence check, not a behavioral proof of the median/threshold logic.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 (`pytest … -k flip`) and AC 5 (`jq … fp_control.json`); Scope — `e3_metrics.py: fp_control_eval`
scenarios: A bug in the median-over-3-replays calculation (e.g., sorting error, off-by-one on the >3/30 threshold) produces a wrong `r1_pass` boolean — the jq schema check passes green while the gate decision is silently incorrect
Edge case: all 3 replays return 0 new blocking findings but `new_advisory_median_delta` is borderline — the threshold comparison logic is untested
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add `fp_control_eval` explicitly to the golden-test list alongside `criterion_deltas` and `pass_to_block_flip_rate`. In AC 4, extend the pytest invocation to `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k 'delta or flip or fp_control' -q` and describe a minimal 3-element replay fixture that covers both threshold conditions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a golden behavioral test (e.g., `-k fp_control`) in `test_e3_metrics.py` covering `fp_control_eval` with a small N=3 fixture: (a) all thresholds pass → `r1_pass=True`, (b) `new_blocking_median>3/30` → `r1_pass=False`, (c) `new_advisory_median_delta>1` → `r1_pass=False`, and wire it to the AC 5 proving command.
evidence: Plan lists: 'e3_metrics.py (pure, LLM-free functions: build_matrix, criterion_deltas, pass_to_block_flip_rate, fp_control_eval)' — all four are pure and offline-testable
AC 4: `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k delta -q` and `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k flip -q` — covers criterion_deltas and pass_to_block_flip_rate, but NOT fp_control_eval
AC 5: `jq -e 'has("new_blocking_median") and has("new_advisory_median_delta") …'` — schema presence check only, not behavioral assertion
ABSENCE: no `-k fp_control` or equivalent behavioral test for fp_control_eval appears anywhere in the plan
fp_control_eval encodes the critical threshold comparisons: median(new_blocking)>3/30 and median(new_advisory_delta)>1 → r1_pass boolean
findings.finding: `fp_control_eval` — the function that computes the R1 gate decision (`new_blocking_median`, `new_advisory_median_delta`, `r1_pass`) — is listed as a named pure function in `e3_metrics.py` alongside `build_matrix`, `criterion_deltas`, and `pass_to_block_flip_rate`, but no `-k fp_control` behavioral golden test is specified. AC #5's proving command is only a `jq` schema-presence check on `fp_control.json`, not a behavioral proof of the median/threshold logic.
findings.location: Scope — `e3_metrics.py: fp_control_eval`; Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 (`pytest … -k flip`) and AC 5 (`jq … fp_control.json`)
scenarios: A sorting error in the N=3 replays median computation (e.g., Python's statistics.median on a length-3 list returning middle element correctly, but the plan failing to specify how ties are broken) produces a wrong `r1_pass` boolean — the jq schema check passes green while the gate decision is silently wrong.
Off-by-one on the >3/30 threshold (e.g., using ≥3 instead of >3) means 3 flipped plans incorrectly FAILs R1; the AC passes green.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the full key schema of `transition_matrix.json` in the plan (including whether it carries a top-level `flip_rate` field for the full sample), and add a `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") …' …/<label>/transition_matrix.json` proving step to AC #2 to close the gap.
evidence: 'flip_rate = |{PASS→BLOCK}| / |{tickets with baseline_verdict == PASS, included}| — Recorded per candidate; the epic bar is ≤0.02' (Primary metric section — implies full ~200-sample denominator)
AC #2 jq: `(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total` — checks arithmetic, not flip_rate presence
AC #4 jq: `jq -e 'has("flip_rate") and (.flip_rate|type=="number")' …/fp_control.json` — reads from 30-plan file only

Scope lists per-candidate files as `raw_verdicts.jsonl, transition_matrix.json, criterion_deltas.jsonl, criterion_deltas_summary.json, fp_control.json` — no key schema for `transition_matrix.json` is provided
ABSENCE: no jq or schema definition specifies whether transition_matrix.json carries a top-level `flip_rate` field
findings.finding: The `transition_matrix.json` schema is never enumerated. The plan names it as the artifact carrying the full-sample (~200-plan) primary metric, and AC #2's `jq` checks cell-arithmetic consistency using `cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block + …`, but neither the plan's What section nor AC #2 specifies whether `transition_matrix.json` carries a top-level `flip_rate` field. AC #4's `jq` proving command for `flip_rate` reads from `fp_control.json` (the 30-plan file), leaving the primary metric's storage location ambiguous.
findings.location: What — 'Primary metric (PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2%)'; Scope — per-candidate file list; AC #2 and AC #4 proving commands
scenarios: Implementer writes `flip_rate` into `transition_matrix.json` (natural choice); AC #4's jq on `fp_control.json` fails even though the primary metric is correctly computed.

Implementer writes `flip_rate` only into `fp_control.json` (to satisfy AC #4) using the 30-plan denominator; the primary full-sample metric is never stored in a committed artifact and is unretrievable post-run.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step to AC #3 (or a new AC) that verifies `criterion_deltas_summary.json` exists and carries the expected per-criterion aggregated fields (e.g., via a jq schema check mirroring the one for `criterion_deltas.jsonl`).
evidence: What: 'A rollup runs/e3/<candidate>/criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion across the sample'
Scope: 'criterion_deltas_summary.json' is listed as a per-candidate output file
AC #3 prove commands check only criterion_deltas.jsonl and pytest -k delta — no jq or existence check for criterion_deltas_summary.json
findings.finding: The Scope and What sections both describe `criterion_deltas_summary.json` as a named deliverable ('A rollup ... aggregates the same fields per criterion across the sample'), but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies this file. AC #3 verifies only `criterion_deltas.jsonl` and the golden unit test for `criterion_deltas`.
findings.location: What §'Per-criterion finding delta schema'; Scope file list; AC #3
scenarios: Implementer omits criterion_deltas_summary.json or produces it with incorrect aggregation; no AC catches the gap, so the R-row attributability use-case silently fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #3's proving block: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC #6's grep proving commands to cover the INDETERMINATE-exclusion rule and the LLM-nondeterminism control, e.g., add `grep -q "INDETERMINATE" ...README.md && grep -q "nondeterminism" ...README.md` (or equivalent key terms).
evidence: Scope: 'an E3 section documenting the method (baseline source + chosen round, cleanly-closed filter, material-fingerprint drift control, INDETERMINATE-exclusion rule, and LLM-nondeterminism control + cost)'
AC #6 grep checks: 'material_fingerprint', 'cleanly-closed', 'all_review_results' — no grep for 'INDETERMINATE' or 'nondeterminism' (or 'temperature', 'null.*floor', etc.)
findings.finding: AC #6 (README documentation) verifies only three grep targets (`material_fingerprint`, `cleanly-closed`, `all_review_results`) but the Scope lists five documentation requirements for the README E3 section: baseline source + chosen round, cleanly-closed filter, material-fingerprint drift control, INDETERMINATE-exclusion rule, and LLM-nondeterminism control with billable-cost note. The INDETERMINATE-exclusion rule and the LLM-nondeterminism control are not covered by any grep in the proving command.
findings.location: Scope §README; AC #6 proving grep commands
scenarios: README is written without the INDETERMINATE-exclusion or nondeterminism-control sections; AC #6 passes anyway, leaving future readers of the experiment without the documented methodology for those design choices.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two grep checks to AC #6: `grep -q "INDETERMINATE" docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q "null.*floor\|nondeterminism" docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the batch driver handles exceptions thrown by `review_plan()` (e.g., catch-log-record-as-EXCLUDED, or catch-retry-N-times-then-exclude), distinct from the INDETERMINATE verdict path, so the run is always resumable and the excluded block is complete.
evidence: Plan states: 'A candidate verdict that is INDETERMINATE (LLM outage / mid-run degradation — §5.1 shows ~8% of reviews land INDETERMINATE)' — INDETERMINATE is treated as the sole failure mode
No mention of try/except, retry, or skip-with-logging for exceptions raised by `review_plan()`
The harness calls the production entrypoint directly: `rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(tid, repo_root=…)`
findings.finding: The harness spec only addresses INDETERMINATE as the failure mode for LLM outage, but does not specify how the batch driver handles hard exceptions (e.g., HTTP 5xx, timeout, unexpected exception) thrown by `review_plan()`. If the production call raises rather than returning INDETERMINATE, no catch/retry/skip logic is defined, leaving the checkpoint in a potentially inconsistent state and the run unrecoverable without manual intervention.
findings.location: What → The replay (batch driver spec) and LLM nondeterminism control section
scenarios: Network timeout raises an exception mid-batch; the atomic-append checkpoint has a partial write; resume re-processes the same ticket but the previous partial record remains, breaking the cardinality assertion
A transient API error on ticket 150/200 halts the entire 200-ticket run with no recovery path
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `review_plan()` call in a try/except that catches all non-INDETERMINATE exceptions, appends a synthetic `{verdict: 'ERROR', error: str(e)}` record to `raw_verdicts.jsonl`, counts it in the `excluded.error` sub-bucket, and continues — preserving the cardinality invariant and resumability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an exception-handling protocol for the batch driver: wrap each `review_plan()` call in a try/except that catches all non-INDETERMINATE exceptions, appends a synthetic `{verdict: 'ERROR', error: str(e), ticket_id: tid}` record to `raw_verdicts.jsonl`, counts it in `excluded.error`, and continues — distinct from the INDETERMINATE verdict path.
evidence: 'A candidate verdict that is INDETERMINATE (LLM outage / mid-run degradation — §5.1 shows ~8% of reviews land INDETERMINATE)' — plan treats INDETERMINATE as the only failure mode
The plan requires: 'assert len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample) before any summary is written' — hard exceptions violate this without a catch
`review_plan()` raises (not returns INDETERMINATE) on a hard context-assembly failure (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:410-411: 'Raises only on a hard context-assembly failure')
ABSENCE: no try/except, retry policy, or explicit 'record-as-ERROR-and-continue' protocol is specified for the batch driver
findings.finding: The batch driver specification designates INDETERMINATE as the sole LLM failure mode but does not specify a fallback when `review_plan()` raises a hard exception (network timeout, HTTP 5xx, unexpected error). The plan's atomic-append checkpoint design requires a record per ticket; an unhandled exception produces neither a record nor a clean exclusion, breaking the `len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample)` cardinality assertion and making the run unresumable.
findings.location: What — 'The replay' paragraph; 'LLM nondeterminism control' section
scenarios: A transient network error on ticket 150/200 raises an exception; the checkpoint has 149 records; resuming re-processes ticket 150 with no indication of the prior failure; the cardinality assertion fails at 201 records.
A ticket in the sample has a corrupt sidecar that raises during `assemble_context`; the batch halts with no recovery path and a truncated `raw_verdicts.jsonl` that cannot be used for analysis.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the checkpoint resume deduplication strategy in the driver spec: e.g., 'on startup, read existing `raw_verdicts.jsonl` (skipping any malformed trailing line), build a `seen_ticket_ids` set, and skip those tickets in the iteration loop.'
evidence: Plan states: 'checkpoint per ticket (atomic append) so a mid-run stop resumes without re-processing'
'asserts `len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample)` before any summary is written'
No specification of how 'without re-processing' is implemented — no mention of a seen-set, line-count check, or ticket_id index
findings.finding: The checkpoint resume logic is described as 'atomic append' per ticket but does not specify how a resumed run detects and skips already-processed tickets. Without an explicit deduplication check (e.g., read existing `raw_verdicts.jsonl` and build a `seen_ticket_ids` set before iterating), a crash mid-append could leave a partial record that is double-counted on resume, causing the `len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample)` assertion to fail or, worse, silently produce a matrix with a duplicate row.
findings.location: What → The replay section; Scope → `replay_candidate.py` driver
scenarios: Process crashes after writing a partial JSONL line for ticket T; on resume the driver re-processes T and appends a second record; the cardinality assert fires or the ticket appears twice in the matrix
findings.suggested_fix: On driver startup for `run` subcommand: open `raw_verdicts.jsonl` in append mode, read all complete lines (catching JSONDecodeError on the last line to handle partial writes), build `seen = {r['ticket_id'] for r in parsed}`, and iterate `[t for t in sample if t['ticket_id'] not in seen]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note (in the delta schema section and README.md) clarifying whether norm_id is stable under rubric criterion renames, and if not, specify how the driver handles criterion-ID drift between baseline and candidate builds (e.g., a criterion-alias map, a separate 'criterion_renamed' flag in the delta row, or a documented caveat that criterion_deltas is descriptive-only when rubric IDs change).
evidence: "norm_id (sidecar.py:261, reword-tolerant, criterion-scoped)" — the plan relies on norm_id stability across builds
"R1 (asserted-capability grounding probe) edits the Pass-1 finder rubric" — Why section
"Findings join across the two builds on norm_id" — delta schema section
ABSENCE: no discussion of what happens to norm_id matching when the rubric criteria themselves are renamed/restructured by the candidate
findings.finding: The per-criterion delta computation joins baseline and candidate findings on `norm_id` (described as 'reword-tolerant, criterion-scoped'), but R1 — the primary candidate under test — explicitly 'edits the Pass-1 finder rubric,' which may rename or restructure criterion IDs. If R1 changes criterion names that are encoded in norm_id, every baseline finding will appear as removed and every candidate finding as new, making the criterion_deltas artifact misleading (100% churn) rather than attributable. This coupling between the delta schema and sidecar's norm_id construction under rubric mutation is unacknowledged.
findings.location: What — Per-criterion finding delta schema / `new_norm_ids` / `removed_norm_ids` computation; sidecar.py:261 norm_id reference
scenarios: R1 renames a rubric criterion (e.g., 'C-SCOPE' → 'C-SCOPE-V2'); all findings citing the old criterion have norm_ids that no longer match the candidate's norm_ids; criterion_deltas shows everything as net-new/removed rather than a meaningful transition — the primary attribution artifact is corrupted for exactly the candidate type this experiment targets.
findings.suggested_fix: In the delta schema definition, add a sentence such as: 'If the candidate modifies criterion IDs, norm_ids from the baseline (old criteria) will not match candidate norm_ids; in that case new_norm_ids and removed_norm_ids reflect criterion-scope churn, not finding-level churn. The driver emits a warning and sets a top-level `criterion_id_drift: true` flag in criterion_deltas_summary.json when any criterion present in baseline findings is absent from the candidate rubric registry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-candidate lock file (e.g., `runs/e3/<candidate>/.lock`) or document a 'one active run per candidate label at a time' operational constraint, so concurrent invocations fail fast rather than silently corrupting `raw_verdicts.jsonl`.
evidence: Plan states per-candidate output goes to `runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl` with no locking or exclusive-access mechanism
'checkpoint per ticket (atomic append)' — atomic per single writer, but no exclusion of concurrent writers
No mention of a lock file, PID file, or 'one run at a time' operator constraint
findings.finding: The plan does not specify ownership or access control for per-candidate output files (`raw_verdicts.jsonl`, etc.) under concurrent invocations of the same candidate label. Two operators running `replay_candidate.py run --candidate R1` simultaneously would both append to the same `raw_verdicts.jsonl`, producing duplicates and an inflated cardinality that breaks the `len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample)` assertion or silently corrupts the matrix.
findings.location: What → The replay section; Scope → `runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl`
scenarios: CI and a developer both trigger an R1 replay run simultaneously; both write to the same file; the analyze step sees 400 rows for a 200-ticket sample and the cardinality assert fires
findings.suggested_fix: At driver startup for `run` subcommand, attempt to create `runs/e3/<candidate>/.lock` exclusively (O_CREAT|O_EXCL); fail with a clear error if it exists. Remove the lock on clean exit or signal handler.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a module-level docstring to `e3_metrics.py` explicitly stating that the separation from `replay_candidate.py` exists to keep all metric computation pure/LLM-free so `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py` can run offline in CI without mocking — this documents the Rule-of-Three exception (testability over call-site count) so the boundary isn't collapsed.
evidence: grep for 'build_matrix', 'criterion_deltas', 'pass_to_block_flip_rate', 'fp_control_eval' across the full codebase returns zero existing call-sites — these are net-new symbols.
The plan's AC proving commands invoke these via 'pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k delta -q' and '-k flip -q' — exactly 2 call-sites total (driver + test), below Rule-of-Three.
The plan states the split exists so functions are 'pure, LLM-free' and CI-collectable — a testability justification tied to the current done-definition (AC3, AC4), not a hypothetical reuse.
findings.finding: The plan splits `e3_metrics.py` as a separate pure-function module from `replay_candidate.py`, creating `build_matrix`, `criterion_deltas`, `pass_to_block_flip_rate`, and `fp_control_eval` as exported symbols. Each has only 2 call-sites (the driver + the test module), below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a new abstraction. The split is justified by testability, but the plan should explicitly acknowledge why the module boundary exists rather than inline functions, to avoid a future reader deleting it as premature.
findings.location: Scope section: 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e3_metrics.py (pure, LLM-free functions: build_matrix, criterion_deltas, pass_to_block_flip_rate, fp_control_eval)'
scenarios: A reviewer or future contributor sees 4 functions each with exactly 2 call-sites and flags the module as premature abstraction, removing the separation and breaking the offline re-derive proving commands.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Scope description: 'e3_metrics.py carries a module docstring explaining the separation exists for offline-testability (no LLM mocks required), satisfying the Rule-of-Three exception for testability-driven pure-function extraction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan (or mitigate in `e3_metrics.py`) that `norm_id` is criteria-scoped, so rubric changes that alter a finding's `criteria[]` will produce different `norm_ids` for the same defect — and specify whether the 'new blocking finding' count should fall back to `fix_unit_key` (criteria-free) matching when criteria-set changes are expected.
evidence: norm_id: `basis = ' '.join(tokens) + '|' + ','.join(sorted(finding.get('criteria', []) or []))` (sidecar.py:268)
norm_id is criteria-scoped by construction — same finding text + different criteria set = different norm_id
R1 amends the G6/E4 rubric; amended rubric may add criteria co-citations to existing findings
fix_unit_key (sidecar.py:279-290) is explicitly criteria-FREE and would be stable across criteria-set changes
Plan does not acknowledge this failure mode or provide a mitigation
findings.finding: `norm_id` (sidecar.py:261-269) hashes `sorted(significant_tokens(finding_text)) + sorted(criteria)`. R1 specifically extends the G6/E4 rubric, which may cause an existing finding that previously cited `['G6']` to additionally cite `['A1']` or `['E4']` under the amended criteria set — producing a different `norm_id` for the same underlying defect. The plan's `new_norm_ids` / `removed_norm_ids` join and the 'new blocking finding' count will treat this as the baseline finding being removed and a new one appearing, inflating the apparent churn and producing a pessimistic flip-rate estimate even when R1 doesn't surface any genuinely new defects.
findings.location: What — 'Primary R1 gate' paragraph: '"New" = a candidate surfaced finding whose `norm_id` is absent from that ticket's frozen baseline surfaced findings.' and 'Per-criterion finding delta schema' section.
scenarios: R1 expands an existing G6 finding to also cite A1. The norm_id changes. baseline reports it as a G6 finding; candidate reports it as a G6+A1 finding. The delta logic counts it as 1 removed + 1 new, overstating churn.

A previously advisory G6+criteria finding gets re-cited under R1's new capability probe criterion. If the new norm_id lands in the blocking bucket, it's counted as a 'new blocking finding' even though it's the same defect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether `norm_id`'s criteria-list component is compatible with cross-build joining when R1 changes criterion attribution: either switch criterion_deltas joining to the criteria-free `fix_unit_key` (sidecar.py:279-290, which is tolerant to criterion-list changes) or document that norm_id matching is criteria-list-sensitive and that criterion re-attribution counts as churn by design.
evidence: Plan: 'Findings join across the two builds on norm_id (the exact-text id differs across builds by construction — sidecar.py:247-257).'
sidecar.py:281-282: 'norm_id cannot serve here — it bakes the sorted criteria list into its hash, so one defect co-cited by N criteria mints N distinct norm_ids.'
sidecar.py:268: 'basis = " ".join(tokens) + "|" + ",".join(sorted(finding.get("criteria", []) or []))'

R1 edits the Pass-1 finder rubric, which could change which criteria a finding is attributed to, not just its text — making criteria-list changes a predictable output of the very candidate under test.
findings.finding: The plan asserts norm_id correctly joins 'the same underlying defect across builds' for the criterion_deltas attribution — but sidecar.py:261-269 shows norm_id hashes the sorted criteria list together with the finding text. If R1's amended rubric causes a finding to be emitted under a different criterion set (e.g., the new asserted-capability criterion instead of E4), the norm_id changes even for textually identical findings, so the join misclassifies a re-attributed finding as a new+removed pair and inflates 'new_norm_ids'/'removed_norm_ids' counts — directly contaminating the per-criterion attribution that the plan cites as the key output for 'attributing churn to specific criteria'.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-criterion finding delta schema' and 'Verdict → matrix mapping'; sidecar.py:261-269, 281-282
scenarios: R1 adds an asserted-capability criterion; a finding previously emitted under E4 is now emitted under the new criterion instead. norm_id differs. criterion_deltas shows a spurious 'removed' E4 finding and a 'new' R1 finding, misattributing what is really a criterion relabeling as new churn.
A finding is co-cited under {E4, G6} by baseline but only {E4} by candidate (or vice versa). norm_id differs; the join fails to match them.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `sidecar.fix_unit_key` (the criteria-free location+claim fingerprint at sidecar.py:279-290) as the join key for criterion_deltas, reserving norm_id for intra-criterion dedup. This matches findings across builds even when R1 changes which criterion a defect is attributed to, making attribution genuinely reflect new surfaced defects rather than criterion-relabeling noise.

findings.finding: 'Cleanly-closed tickets' is used in AC3 as the denominator for the primary <=2% gate metric but is never defined in the plan — an executor cannot identify which tickets qualify without an external definition or a reference to one.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'The PASS->BLOCK flip rate on cleanly-closed tickets is computed and recorded per candidate'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a definition sentence to AC3: 'Cleanly-closed tickets are defined as reviews in the E1 corpus whose final recorded verdict was PASS with no subsequent reopen or escalation flag (corpus field: `status=closed_clean`).' Adjust field name to match actual corpus schema.

findings.finding: The plan uses the term 'cleanly-closed tickets' as the denominator for the primary <=2% PASS->BLOCK gate metric (Why section and AC3), but this term is never defined anywhere in the plan, the codebase, or the named ADR — no grep or read probe finds a definition of 'cleanly-closed'.
findings.location: Why section: 'the epic's gate requires <=2% PASS->BLOCK flips on cleanly-closed tickets'; AC3: 'PASS->BLOCK flip rate on cleanly-closed tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a definitions subsection stating: 'A ticket is cleanly closed if it satisfies post_claim_edit_class=="none" AND reopen_count==0 AND force_close==false in the E1 outcome corpus.' Align this with the <=2% gate row's stated intent.

findings.finding: 'Cleanly-closed tickets' is used in AC3 as the denominator for the primary <=2% PASS->BLOCK flip rate, but the term is never defined in the plan. The outcome_corpus.jsonl has a closed `post_claim_edit_class` vocabulary (plan-authored-post-claim, operator-attested-retag, ac-strengthened, substantive-unclassified, cosmetic, none) plus `reopen_count` and `force_close`, but 'cleanly-closed' maps to none of these values unambiguously. An executor cannot identify which tickets qualify as the denominator.
findings.location: AC3: 'The PASS->BLOCK flip rate on cleanly-closed tickets is computed and recorded per candidate'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'cleanly-closed tickets' with an explicit filter expression, e.g.: 'tickets where post_claim_edit_class == "none" AND reopen_count == 0 AND force_close == False (no substantive post-claim edits, no reopens, no force-closes).' Commit the filter as part of the replay driver so the denominator is encoded, not inferred.

findings.finding: The transition matrix requires a BASELINE verdict (the PASS/BLOCK verdict the current/pre-candidate gate produced for each sampled ticket), but the plan names no source for this baseline column. The E1 outcome corpus contains no verdict field; the plan does not specify whether baseline verdicts come from existing REVIEW_RESULT sidecars, a fresh control run, or another artifact.
findings.location: What section: 'compute the verdict-transition matrix (PASS->PASS/PASS->BLOCK/BLOCK->PASS/BLOCK->BLOCK)'; AC2: 'commits the verdict-transition matrix'

findings.finding: The verdict-transition matrix (PASS->PASS / PASS->BLOCK / BLOCK->PASS / BLOCK->BLOCK) requires a baseline verdict per ticket, but the E1 outcome_corpus.jsonl has no `verdict` field, and the plan only describes executing the *candidate* branch build over the sample — the source of the baseline (pre-candidate) verdict is unspecified.
findings.location: What section / AC2: 'The replay executes the candidate branch build over the full sample and commits the verdict-transition matrix'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit step: 'Run the production (main-branch) build over the same ~200-ticket sample first to establish baseline verdicts; then run the candidate build and diff (baseline_verdict, candidate_verdict) per ticket, segmented by IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION. Both runs must use the same IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION cohort to satisfy ADR 0036 non-pooling.'

findings.finding: The verdict-transition matrix (PASS->PASS / PASS->BLOCK / BLOCK->PASS / BLOCK->BLOCK) is a core output, but the plan never defines where the BASELINE verdict per ticket comes from. The E1 outcome_corpus.jsonl schema (confirmed in runs/README.md) has no `verdict` field — only post_claim_edit_class, reopen_count, force_close, review_round_count, etc. Without specifying whether the baseline is (a) recovered from the REVIEW_RESULT sidecars already stamped on each ticket, (b) a separate baseline run of the current gate over the sample, or (c) some other source, an executor cannot compute the matrix.
findings.location: What section / AC2: 'commits the verdict-transition matrix'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Baseline acquisition' sub-step to the replay method: run the current (unmodified) gate over the ~200 sampled tickets first, store each verdict as `baseline_verdict ∈ {PASS, BLOCK}`, and commit this as the baseline column. Alternatively, recover verdicts from the most-recent REVIEW_RESULT sidecar per ticket and define a mapping of INDETERMINATE→excluded.

findings.finding: The plan requires 'per-criterion finding deltas' in AC2 but never defines the delta schema. The production_batch_runner emits a findings list and a batch_plan coverage dict per run; the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar stores per-criterion finding counts and fingerprints. But 'delta' is ambiguous: it could mean (a) count(new findings per criterion) − count(baseline findings per criterion), (b) a set-diff by finding fingerprint, (c) a boolean flip per criterion, or (d) something else. No output format or co-located processing rule ties this to the transition matrix schema.
findings.location: AC2: 'with per-criterion finding deltas' / What section: 'Record per-criterion finding deltas alongside verdict flips'

findings.finding: The replay runs live LLM calls with no stated strategy for controlling LLM nondeterminism between the candidate run and the baseline. The plan's primary gate criterion is a PASS→BLOCK flip rate ≤2% on a ~200-ticket sample. Without temperature control or replication, natural sampling variance across two runs of the same model on the same input can produce a non-trivial number of verdict flips that are attributable to the LLM's stochastic output rather than the candidate change — potentially causing a good candidate to fail the 2% gate or a bad one to pass it.
findings.location: What section / AC2 / AC3

findings.finding: The replay runs the real pipeline (live LLM calls) over ~200 tickets, but the plan specifies no fallback protocol for individual ticket runs that produce an INDETERMINATE verdict (LLMUnavailableError, mid-run failure, partial run). The gate_dispatch code has three explicit INDETERMINATE degradation paths (LLMUnavailableError, verify failure, finder failure). Without a defined handling rule, the flip-rate denominator and matrix cell counts are ambiguous when any sample tickets fail.
findings.location: What section / AC2–AC3: matrix and flip rate computation
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line protocol to the What section: 'INDETERMINATE verdicts (LLM outage or mid-run failure) are excluded from the matrix and the flip-rate computation; the exclusion count is reported per candidate run.' Record this in the replay driver so it is enforced, not left to the analyst.

findings.finding: The stratification method is not specified: the plan lists four strata dimensions (ticket type, overlay triggers, verdict, model) but does not say how to allocate the ~200 samples across them (proportional to corpus distribution, equal per stratum, or fixed minimum per cell). This is a defaultable but real methodological decision.
findings.location: What section: 'Stratification: ticket type, overlay triggers, verdict, model.'

findings.finding: The transition matrix requires a 'before' (baseline) verdict for each ticket, but the plan never specifies its source. The candidate re-run calls `assemble_context(req.target_ticket)` which reads the CURRENT live ticket text; if the baseline comes from persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecars (which captured the verdict at original-review time), tickets with post-claim edits (~8.3% of reviewed work tickets) will have different plan text in the two columns — inflating spurious PASS→BLOCK flips unrelated to the candidate change. If instead the baseline is a separate fresh re-run of the current (non-candidate) build, the cost doubles and LLM nondeterminism is compounded across both runs.
findings.location: What section / AC2 ('executes the candidate branch build over the full sample and commits the verdict-transition matrix')
findings.suggested_fix: The most pragmatic approach: read baseline verdicts from persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecars (already available, zero extra LLM cost), and restrict the 'cleanly-closed' PASS→BLOCK denominator to tickets whose `post_claim_edit_class == 'none'` so that plan-text drift cannot inflate the flip count. Document this explicitly in the method writeup (AC4).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
